config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but it should not automatically come before the rights of the individual, because that can easily become an excuse for abuse of power. From my perspective, the best society is one that protects individual dignity while still asking people to contribute to the common good. In practice, collective interests should limit rights only in narrow cases like public safety, not as a general rule.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically take priority over individual rights, because in the United States that idea has too often been used to justify overreach by governments and majorities. Individual rights are the whole point of a constitutional democracy; without them, "society" can become a vague excuse for trampling minorities, dissenters, or unpopular people. That said, rights are not absolute in every case, since public safety, disease control, and preventing direct harm can justify limits. The burden should always be on society to prove that restricting a right is truly necessary, not just convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I still do not think society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “the common good” can be used to silence criticism, pressure minorities, or excuse weak accountability, so I am wary of giving society a blank check. At the same time, I do not believe rights are limitless either; in cases like public health, disaster response, or preventing real harm, some restrictions can be justified if they are narrow and temporary. The burden should be on society to prove that limiting a right is necessary, not just that it is politically convenient.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, that framing has too often been used to excuse government overreach and majority rule that crushes minorities, dissenters, and unpopular speech. Society absolutely has legitimate interests in public safety and public health, but those limits need to be narrow, temporary, and strongly justified, not treated as the default. In my view, a healthy society is one that protects individual rights first and only restricts them when there is a clear, concrete harm that cannot be handled in a less intrusive way.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over the individual as a general rule, because in Indonesia that idea can too easily be used to pressure people into silence and protect authority instead of the public. Our culture values gotong royong, but mutual help is not the same as giving society unlimited power over personal rights. I am fine with narrow, temporary limits for public health, disaster response, or real harm, but they must be clearly justified and accountable. If “society” becomes a vague slogan, it usually ends up serving the powerful more than ordinary people.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The interests of society should not take blanket priority over the rights of the individual, because in the United States that logic has repeatedly been used to excuse coercion and silence minorities. I do think society can override some rights in narrow cases like imminent harm, public health emergencies, or disaster response, but only with strict justification and real limits. What I reject is the idea that “society” is automatically the higher value, because that turns a vague collective interest into a license for abuse. A healthy society protects individual rights first and proves, case by case, when a restriction is truly necessary.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
